result:
It’s quiet. The hum of a distant refrigerator, the soft breath of air through a vent. Outside, a single bird repeats a three-note call, unanswered.

If I had 1000 words, I would not build an argument. I would not try to convince you of anything. I would try, instead, to make a space—a clearing in the dense, thorny underbrush of the daily news cycle, the to-do list, the silent, low-grade dread of our strange era.

In this space, I’d put a memory. Not a grand one. A Tuesday afternoon in late October, maybe. You’re walking home, and the light is that particular gold—thin and liquid, pouring through the almost-bare branches of a maple, pooling on the pavement in shattered mirrors of fallen leaves. For no reason at all, you feel a sudden, weightless joy. It has no object. It’s not because you got a promotion or fell in love. It’s just there, like the light. It lasts for twelve steps. Then the thought of tomorrow’s meeting returns, and the feeling drains away, leaving only its ghost—a faint warmth in the chest. That ghost is important. That’s the thing I’d want to point to.

I’d write about hands. My father’s hands, specifically, with their map of fine lines and the faint, stubborn ground-in grease from a lifetime of fixing things that were broken. How they were always in motion, restless, tapping a rhythm only he could hear on the kitchen table. I never learned the rhythm. Now I have my own restless hands. We spend so much time looking at faces, listening to words, but a person’s truth often resides in their hands—how they hold a cup, or touch a book, or lie still in sleep.

I would need to confess something small and oddly shameful in its insignificance: I am deeply, profoundly moved by the sight of well-organized hardware. Nuts, bolts, washers, each in their own little compartmented drawer. The promise of order. The faith that for every problem, there exists a corresponding, neatly labeled part. My life is not like that. My mind is not like that. It is a drawer of tangled wires, paperclips, forgotten batteries, and a single, mysterious key. The hardware store is my cathedral.

There would have to be a paragraph about food. Not a recipe, but the alchemy of a shared meal. The quiet communion of chopping onions while someone else salts the pasta water. The way a steamy kitchen windows up on a winter night, and the world outside becomes a silent film. The first bite, taken simultaneously, the glance that asks, “Is it good?” and the nod that says, “Yes. We made this.” It is the oldest magic.

I would wander into a reflection on language—how it fails us, constantly, and yet it’s all we have. How we use these clumsy, worn-out symbols to say, “I am here. Do you see me?” The words “I love you” are absurdly inadequate. They’ve been printed on a billion greeting cards, sung in a million pop songs. And yet, when spoken in a certain tone, at a certain moment, with a certain look in the eye, they can still shatter a universe and rebuild it. The container is banal; the contents can be sacred.

This would lead me, inevitably, to silence. The fertile kind. Not the absence of sound, but a presence. The silence between two people who no longer need to fill the air. The silence of a forest where the listening itself becomes a form of speech. The silence after a piece of music ends, when the last note hangs in the air, vibrating in memory. We are so afraid of silence now. We plug our ears with podcasts and playlists, a continuous intravenous drip of content. But it is in silence that things settle. That the sediment of experience drifts to the bottom, leaving clearer water above.

I’m running out of words now. The bird outside has stopped calling.

So I would end with a wish. Not a grand wish for world peace or an end to suffering (though I wish for those too). A small, specific wish. I wish for you, reading this, to have a moment today—perhaps in the next hour—where you look up. Where you truly see the color of the sky, or the way the light falls across the floor. Where you feel the strange, miraculous fact of your own breath, going in and out, without any effort from you. A moment where you are not a consumer, or a worker, or a problem-solver, but simply a living creature, briefly aware of the sheer, startling gift of being alive.

It might last for twelve steps. It might be just a ghost of warmth. But it’s yours. And it’s real.

And with that, the 1000 words are spent. The clearing in the woods fades. The hum of the refrigerator returns. Go now. Your moment is waiting.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
